questions.question: What is the first thing to clarify before discussing exactly-once delivery?
options: Which side effect boundary the guarantee is supposed to cover in the real system.
Which dashboard color the team prefers.
How many developers like Kafka more than queues.
Whether JSON or Protobuf is more compact.
questions.explanation: Without a named boundary, the guarantee is too vague to evaluate.

questions.question: Why is at-least-once delivery often paired with idempotent consumers?
options: Because retries and crash recovery are practical only if repeated delivery does not repeat the side effect.
Because at-least-once means the broker removes all duplicates.
Because idempotency eliminates the need for acknowledgements.
Because every consumer can safely drop old messages.
questions.explanation: Idempotent consumers are the usual way to make retries safe.

questions.question: Which answer about exactly-once is strongest?
options: The broker supports it, so every downstream side effect is safe automatically.
Exactly-once is only meaningful within a stated boundary, and external side effects still need idempotency or reconciliation.
Exactly-once removes the need to reason about crashes between processing and acknowledgement.
Exactly-once means replay is no longer possible.
questions.explanation: Strong answers name the scope and limits of the guarantee.

questions.question: What failure is most likely if a consumer commits a database write and crashes before ack?
options: The message may be delivered again, so the consumer must suppress duplicate side effects.
The broker permanently loses all future messages.
The partition ordering becomes globally random.
The producer can no longer retry publishes.
questions.explanation: This is the classic duplicate-delivery window in at-least-once systems.

questions.question: Which metric most directly reveals whether deduplication is doing useful work?
options: Duplicate suppression hit rate alongside duplicate delivery rate.
Only total number of schemas in the repo.
Average README size per lesson.
The number of workflow timers created.
questions.explanation: Those metrics show both incoming duplication and successful suppression.

questions.question: Why is very short dedupe retention dangerous?
options: Late retries or planned replay can recreate the side effect after the protection window expires.
It forces all producers to use exactly-once transactions.
It makes consumer lag impossible to measure.
It guarantees message loss on the broker.
questions.explanation: Retention must match realistic retry and replay behavior.

questions.question: When is at-most-once usually acceptable?
options: When the event is low-value and occasional loss costs less than coordination and dedupe overhead.
When the system is charging a customer.
When the only copy of an audit record is on the event bus.
When downstream side effects cannot be repeated safely.
questions.explanation: Low-value telemetry is a common acceptable at-most-once case.

questions.question: What trade-off often appears when moving from best-effort delivery to stronger application-level semantics?
options: You gain clearer correctness under failure, but you pay with state, latency, and operational complexity.
You gain stronger correctness and always reduce cost at the same time.
You eliminate the need for reconciliation tooling.
You remove the need for message IDs.
questions.explanation: Stronger semantics are useful, but they are never free.
